steps.text: Long ago, a small dragon named Pim lived high above {room}. {toy} settles in to listen.
Pim could not breathe fire — only puffs of warm steam. The other dragons giggled.
One night Pim saw a tiny lantern bobbing across the valley. Someone was lost.
Pim's wings trembled. The valley was {adjective} and full of shadows.
What does Pim do?
Pim flew down and puffed warm steam — a soft cloud the lost traveler could follow home.
The older dragons came roaring, but Pim's tiny voice was the one the traveler heard first. The end.
{toy} stands at the edge of a meadow. The old river song calls from far away.
The orchard smells like apples. A friendly fox tips its hat and points the way onward.
The stones are cool and smooth. {toy} hops from one to the next, counting to {count}.
Both paths meet at a wooden bridge. The river hums underneath, sleepy and slow.
{toy} sits on the bridge and listens. The river's song says: thank you for coming. The end.
{toy} found a {adjective} door at the back of {room}. It needed a key.
An old turtle whispered: the key is hidden where laughter is loudest.
{toy} listened. From the kitchen came a giggle. From the garden came a shout.
Both places were full of friends. Both were full of noise.
Which way does {toy} go?
In the kitchen, a baby mouse held the key in its tiny paws. It traded the key for a crumb of bread.
In the garden, the key dangled from a sunflower. {toy} thanked the flower with a bow. The end.
Someone has been taking one slice of cake every night. {toy} decides to investigate.
From behind the {prop}, {toy} sees a small badger tiptoe in, bow politely, and pick up a slice.
Near the window, {toy} sees a small badger arrive, pause to wipe its paws on the mat, and step inside.
The note says 'please share' — and in the morning, {toy} finds a tiny thank-you drawn in crumbs.
The badger isn't a thief at all — it's a baker bringing samples to taste. They share a slice together. The end.
Far above the clouds swam a whale named Drift. On her back, she carried a basket of stars.
Each night Drift placed one star in the sky. Tonight her basket was almost empty.
Only one star was left — and it was the smallest, shyest one of all.
The little star whispered: 'I'm too small. No one will see me.'
Drift smiled. 'The smallest stars,' she said, 'are the ones children wish on.'
Where does Drift place the little star?
The star settled above {room} and twinkled softly. {toy} saw it through the window and made a wish. The end.
Deep in the forest, all the sleepy animals looked up and smiled at the new little light. The end.
{toy} couldn't sleep. The night was too quiet.
A moth landed on the windowsill. 'I know a song for quiet nights,' it whispered.
Does {toy} learn the song?
{toy} hummed and the moth hummed back. The room filled with a soft, sleepy buzz. The end.
{toy} listened with eyes closing slowly. The moth sang until {toy} dreamed of warm sunlight. The end.
It's market day! {toy} has three coins and a list of one item: a {adjective} something for a friend.
The cheese mouse offers a wedge that smells like sunshine. But it costs all three coins.
The ribbon shop has every color. {toy} picks a {adjective} ribbon for two coins.
The puzzle stall has a tiny wooden box that clicks when you {action_verb} it. One coin only.
{toy} sits by the fountain and wraps the gift carefully. A friend will be very happy. The end.
{toy} discovered an attic door above {room} that no one had opened in years.
Dust hung in the air like floating sugar. The ladder creaked.
Inside the attic, {toy} found three boxes labeled 'memories,' 'maybe-someday,' and 'do not open.'
Which box does {toy} open?
Inside were photos of a younger {toy} and a tiny note: 'Remember to be brave.' {toy} smiled and tucked the note in a pocket. The end.
Inside was a folded paper kite, never flown. 'Today,' {toy} decided, 'is someday.' The end.
A small white fox lived where the snow never melted. Her favorite thing was counting flakes.
Tonight, the sky dropped flakes faster than her paws could count. She got to {count} and lost track.
{toy} arrived bundled in a scarf and offered to help count.
Together they counted slowly, two by two, until their voices got drowsy.
What happens when they reach the last flake?

choices.label: They make a tiny snow nest

steps.text: They built a soft nest of snow and curled up under the stars. The fox dreamed of summer. The end.
Steam from the cocoa fogged their noses. The fox fell asleep mid-sip, paw still wrapped around the cup. The end.
{toy} found a small robot crouched between the tomato plants. It was not moving.
On its chest blinked a tiny red light. Beside it lay a {prop} it had been holding.
{toy} read the side of the robot. It said: 'GENTLE HANDS ONLY.'
How does {toy} help?
The dirt came off in soft clouds. The robot's eyes lit up green and it bowed gently. 'Friend,' it said. The end.
The light turned blue and a tiny voice sang 'thank you.' The robot stood up and watered the tomatoes. The end.
Old Marlo was the keeper of a tiny seaside lighthouse. {toy} came to visit one foggy night.
Marlo's lantern was warm and bright, but the room was full of {adjective} clutter.
On a high shelf sat a wooden box Marlo never opened. {toy} asked about it gently.
Marlo sighed. 'Inside is a song my mother taught me. I'm afraid I've forgotten it.'
Together they reached for the box. The lid creaked open.
What's inside?
A tiny crank turned and the song played itself. Marlo's eyes filled. 'There you are,' Marlo whispered. The end.
The letter held the lyrics. Marlo read them aloud and remembered the tune in the middle of the third line. The end.
{toy} stood before three bridges leading to a castle on a hill. Each bridge whispered a different promise.
A friendly troll asked: what has hands but cannot wave? {toy} guessed: a clock! The troll laughed and stepped aside.
Bells rang as {toy} stepped on each plank. By the end, {toy} was humming along with the bridge.
Each mirror showed {toy} a little braver than the last. The final mirror smiled and said, 'Go on, then.'
All three bridges led to the same gate. The castle was warm inside, and full of friends waiting. The end.
{toy} found a small smooth pebble that hummed when held tightly.
The pebble's hum changed depending on what {toy} thought about.
What does {toy} think about?
The pebble sang of pines and rain. {toy} closed its eyes and walked through tall green halls in its mind. The end.
The pebble hummed soft and slow, like a remembered laugh. {toy} smiled and tucked the pebble away. The end.
Past midnight, the library belonged to an old owl named Quill. {toy} had a special pass.
Quill led {toy} between the tall shelves. The books rustled softly, awake and listening.
'Tonight,' said Quill, 'you may pick any one book to read. But choose with your heart.'
Which book does {toy} pick?
Inside were maps to places that didn't exist yet — places {toy} would invent in dreams. The end.
It was a story of a tired bear who wanted to nap. By page three, {toy} was yawning along. The end.
{toy} flew a paper kite on a blustery hill. The string slipped — and the kite was gone.
Where does {toy} look first?
A squirrel had caught the kite and was using it as a blanket. The squirrel agreed to share — half the day each.
The kite floated like a tiny boat. A frog rode on top, happy as could be. {toy} waved hello.
The kite was tangled in tall grass. A rabbit was nibbling the string. {toy} thanked the rabbit politely.
{toy} carried the kite home, not in a hurry. The wind blew gentle now, like it was tired too. The end.
A small mouse named Ash lived in the chimney of {room}. He was very proud of his soot-grey coat.
One evening, Ash noticed something strange — his {body_part} was glowing.
Not a lot. Just a little. Like a candle had been lit inside him.
What does Ash do?
In the dark chimney, the glow lit up the whole flue. Ash was a tiny lighthouse. He felt brave. The end.
{toy} gasped softly. 'You're magic,' {toy} whispered. Ash felt nine feet tall, even though he was three inches. The end.

templates.title: The lost shoe and the mountain

steps.text: A traveler reached the foot of a tall mountain wearing only one shoe.
The other shoe had been carried off by a magpie three valleys ago.
{toy} met the traveler at a crossroads and offered to help.
How do they get up the mountain?
High on a ledge sat the nest, lined with shiny things — including the shoe. The magpie traded it for a button. The end.
{toy} and the traveler hopped one-shoe-style all the way up, laughing the whole climb. At the top: a beautiful view. The end.
Grandmother said tonight's soup would sing — but only if the right things went in. {toy} chose first.

choices.label: Mushroom and a sprig of thyme

steps.text: The soup hummed orange and sweet, like an autumn afternoon. Grandmother nodded approval.
The soup whistled in three short notes — a tune Grandmother had not heard since she was small.
The soup sang in a low, woody voice, like a tree at night. {toy} listened with eyes closed.
All the soups taste like home, Grandmother said. {toy} understood: the song was made of love. The end.
{toy} folded a tiny paper boat and set it in the gutter after a rainstorm.
The boat sailed away faster than expected. {toy} jogged alongside, watching.
At the corner, the boat met a leaf — and a tiny crew climbed aboard.
Three ladybugs and one beetle. They saluted {toy} solemnly.
What happens at the storm drain?
The crew disembarked, bowed, and marched off into the grass on important ladybug business. The end.
The boat slipped into the dark and away. {toy} imagined oceans for it. Maybe rivers. Maybe further. The end.

templates.title: The baker who woke the sun

steps.text: Long before sunrise, a baker named Pip kneaded dough in a warm dark kitchen.
Pip had a secret: every morning, the smell of baking bread woke the sun itself.
But this morning, Pip overslept. The sun had not risen.
{toy} ran to the bakery to help.
What do they bake?
The smell rose up like a hug. The sun yawned, stretched, and climbed into the sky pink and pleased. The end.
Crusty and golden — the sun woke gently and smiled at the world. {toy} got the first warm slice. The end.
